data.title: ONEOK: Bullish Setup Remains Despite Slower Near-Term Growth
data.description:
In my December 2025 thesis, I had argued that ONEOK's (OKE) upside outlook was driven by a combination of supporting factors - deleveraging, falling capital intensity, and EBITDA expansion (supported by synergies and operating leverage). The stock was trading at around 9.8x EV to forward EBITDA. I saw a conservative case return potential of up to 55%, primarily driven by an EBITDA expansion toward $8.5b over the next couple of years and a rerating to at least ~12x EV to EBITDA (on lower risk perception, debt reduction, and additional capacities starting to fire).

Since then, the stock has already rallied by 20%, which keeps some of the conservative upside of ~55% still open (conservative because I assumed a two-year window and an inflation-level ~2% growth in EBITDA). Some rerating has also happened, and now the EV to forward EBITDA sits closer to 10.6x.

Q4 earnings have now reported 2025 EBITDA closing at ~$8.02b. The guidance for 2026 is a little more subdued than the trajectory assumed for ~$8.5b in two years ($8.1b at the midpoint). The modest growth outlook is attributed primarily to weaker differentials and moderated producer activity - assumptions that could get challenged as oil prices have settled higher than modeled at the time of the Q4 call (conservative assumptions of ~$55-60 WTI prior to recent geopolitical volatility). Therefore, the scope for upside surprises very much exists - I feel markets are still assigning valuations based on a conservative baseline and have not accounted for a higher oil price regime. The higher oil regime, although introducing volatility (which could compress differentials and reduce earnings visibility/stability), is a net positive in my view. That will support higher producer activity, volumes, and spread expansion - key drivers for a higher than modeled EBITDA at this point.

If the conservative assumptions modeled currently (and potentially reflected in prices) are removed, upside exists from strong internal execution (that has more than offset the weaker macro outlook for 2026) and optionalities like LNG and data centers. That asymmetry means ONEOK is still a Buy. The short-term trajectory to higher multiples and EBITDA could be subject to immediate macro developments to an extent, but the long-term thesis looks very well intact. Even if management's apparently conservative guidance of $8.1b EBITDA at the midpoint for 2026 turns out to be true, there should be enough time for the thesis to work out as favorable conditions return. That is, the worst-case scenario right now is a back-loaded growth story and a pushing out of timelines. All in all, ONEOK is still a Buy both for the short term and, more so, for the long term - I downgrade it from a Strong Buy, though, because part of the thesis is realized (shifting the thesis levers from strong asymmetry to time and execution), and macro volatility is a variable to keep an eye on.

Room for Rerating Still Exists

Looking at the rerating so far from December, EV to forward EBITDA multiples have increased by 0.8. The initial room for rerating was argued as 2.2 (from ~9.8x to 12x). That means the bulk of the rerating potential is yet to be exhausted. Historically, the stock has traded in a 9x-14x range, with ~12x representing a normalized, stable growth multiple - not a very bullish case.

More importantly, this initial rerating has happened without any meaningful EBITDA acceleration or optimistic guidance. Which means markets have likely started to only factor in lower balance sheet risks and integration success of new projects and acquisitions. Earnings expansion and structural growth are yet to start impacting valuations.

Bridging the Flat EBITDA Narrative

Although the $8.1b midpoint EBITDA guidance appears to be flattish, it is not a reversal or even stagnation (~1% growth). Plus, it is part of a broader range, where the upper end of the guidance ($8.3b) could see excellent progress toward my conservative target of ~$8.5b in 2 years. I stay conservative, though, because ONEOK's 2025 number has actually fallen short of the initial guidance of ~$8.225b.

However, the 2025 EBITDA numbers seem to have been dragged down by external and timing factors rather than a lack of structural growth. 2025 volumes were impacted by ~100 MMcf/d lower than expected Bakken activity. Weather effects further impacted volumes (~10% lower in January 2026), and contract roll-offs (~18k bpd in Rockies) added timing pressure. Even the 2026 EBITDA bridge shows internal growth is intact with ~$100m from Permian volume growth and delayed third-party plant volumes, ~$150m from asset optimization and synergies, and continued contributions from acquisitions (Magellan, EnLink, Medallion). What offsets the internal growth in 2026 is mostly external: timing and macro factors. Around ~$150m of impact is expected from lower differentials (e.g., Waha–Katy compression). The ~$85m drop due to lack of one-time gains seen in 2025 (debt repurchases) is a timing and comps issue, not structural. Overall lower realized pricing across G&P, NGL, and refined products is a macro headwind.

Importantly, these macro and external pressures are mostly tied to the oil price environment, not directly, but due to second-order effects on producer activity, spreads, and prices. And the guidance prices are ~$55-60 WTI (and potentially lower spreads and low-single-digit volume growth) in continuation of the Q4 actual realized levels of ~$55-63. That is down by almost 20% from the earlier assumptions of $70+. Since the Q4 earnings call, WTI crude has moved up significantly, which could ideally improve drilling activity and spreads. The path to improvements needs to be seen in light of realistic constraints like infrastructure, a volatile regime where producers can't plan on stable price levels to take strategic decisions, and further clarity on how the Iran situation evolves. However, net-net, I still see the current regime as volatile but more supportive, not weaker than what was modeled at the time of the Q4 call.

Importantly, this potentially better regime and supportive oil prices ahead are seemingly not already factored into prices. ONEOK was trading at ~$82 post results, spiked up to ~$92 at the peak of the spike in energy prices, and has now normalized at ~$86 - that is not far from the post-results and pre-Iran war levels. That means markets are not really assigning excessive optimism to a favorable oil price regime ahead, and if oil prices settle above the current conservative estimates, we could see EBITDA numbers turn up far better than that currently assumed.

Even if the short-term path falters, the path to ~$8.5b and a rerating toward ~12x EV to EBITDA will be delayed, not structurally challenged. In 2027, there are several uplifting factors that could help retain my assumptions, including the ramp-up of Medford fractionation capacity (100k and 110k bpd in two phases), the Bighorn processing plant coming online, and the Denver pipeline expansion - all of which add incremental volume and throughput. Additionally, we have a potential ~$700m of integration synergies yet to play out fully (~$150m already expected in 2026).

Upside Even in Conservative Scenarios

Throughout my thesis, I have not relied on even reasonably bullish assumptions. The earlier thesis did not look at radical EBITDA improvements or rerating, and the current thesis, too, does not rely on a favorable regime in terms of oil prices, although that optionality is highly possible to play out. I also see a delayed but highly likely path to the remainder of the ~55% gains I expected in December last year, in case 2026 EBITDA does not improve from the guidance levels. That is a lot of conservative layers, which still show a favorable return path for a Buy, even at these levels and after a ~20% rally. The downgrade from a Strong Buy is not driven by a weaker thesis, but rather a partial realization of the originally identified valuation upside and a shift in the remaining return drivers from strong asymmetry to time, execution, and macro visibility.

I have also extrapolated current operating conditions and do not heavily rely on several drivers. One such factor is data center demand. ONEOK already reports advanced negotiations with hyperscalers and is looking at potentially multi-year gas demand growth - this helps the long-term thesis and does not seem to be priced in yet, looking at historical EV to EBITDA multiples. Beyond this, LNG and broader industrial demand indicate a more than 5 Bcf/d of demand pipeline, supporting long-duration volume growth. There are some optimization opportunities in ethane recovery, spread capture, and spot volume realization that can capture incremental EBITDA upside. These drivers are already visible; the debate is more centered around timing than potential.

data.title: TechnipFMC: Get Out While The Getting Out Is Good
data.description:
TechnipFMC plc (FTI) services the offshore industry and then the subsea part of the oil and gas industry. This is a service company. That is an extremely cyclical industry that has some fairly fast-moving technology. The whole situation makes keeping a competitive position a real challenge in this industry. Furthermore, every time I point out a technological advance that lowers costs for an upstream company, it likewise shrinks the need for some services in that vast service industry. Many of the industry leaders constantly acquire companies to avoid the industry shrinkage that goes on almost continuously.

Stock Price

The quant system shows this currently as a hold. That alone should be a warning about the situation.

As shown above, the stock price has lately been on quite a run. But this is a cyclical industry in which the price-earnings ratio is now well past 20. That means that this stock has a premium price in an industry that generally shrinks and is cyclical.

This is an industry that has to hope for new technology that saves the upstream business money and expands the market. If history is any guide, then good luck with that one. It has happened, but it is extremely rare for it to happen two years (or more) in a row to cause the outlook for the service industry to be anything but long-term shrinkage.

Furthermore, those market expansion gains rarely come with advance advertising. They are often over before even much of the news gets out that a technological advance happened. It is just that fast (as a rule), and then it is back to the usual long-term market shrinkage.

Smaller companies like this one often have a few bright years for one reason or another. But then they either get acquired or they "flameout". This company is a bit on the expensive side to get acquired.

Upside Potential

Furthermore, the Dallas Federal Reserve Energy Survey reports that most senior executives in the United States are not changing their plans because commodity prices are currently high. That is likely to be the attitude of firms in the industry throughout the world overall. No promises, though.

The reason for this is that most firms require payback certainty and usually then some to account for all the things that happen in the process of expanding operations.

This goes back to the survey's comments from senior executives that would appear to indicate that the industry does not have a long-term plan upon which to base actions from the President of the United States.

There are also industry constraints like midstream, which take years to bring into being when it comes to expansion.

So, it would appear to take quite a bit for the industry to consider expanding production along with some careful political backing that provides enough certainty to entice the industry to expand. Right now, actions would appear to not back expansion (only words).

Accounting

Any time a United States investor heads overseas or out of the country in general, that investor needs to take time to learn the accounting system that prevails. There are also expected industry customs for that accounting interpretation in the area in which the company operates.

Additionally, the oversight of industries in various countries varies tremendously.

All of these considerations add risk to an overseas investment. I do not know how many times something happened in another country that an investor told me would not happen here.

"Buyer beware" comes with an exclamation point when investing overseas.

Financial Results

This company is somewhat helpful in that it does reconcile its results to United States GAAP. That can make at least some items comparable to other United States companies that report under GAAP.

As expected, the final results for the quarter and the fiscal year show a good gain from the previous fiscal year. That is what is driving that extremely high price-earnings ratio shown before.

But the key to making money in this part of the industry is finding a consistent way to take profits while avoiding the cyclical downturns. The cyclical downturns in the service part of the oil and gas industry can be brutal. The offshore part of the industry has some of the most cyclically extreme swings I have ever seen over the years. Oftentimes, pricing takes a big hit when demand becomes soft.

So, it is important to identify when something becomes overheated, as is likely the case with the stock price here. This is not a part of the industry where growth stories last long. Therefore, for me, that price-earnings ratio indicates that the caution exhibited by the Seeking Alpha Website Quant System is more than justified.

Debt

This is a fast-growing situation. That demands a fair amount of cash to fund the growth of working capital as the company grows. But with service companies, when a cyclical downturn happens, working capital often shrinks dramatically to release a lot of cash.

This makes it harder to tell how the company will do during a cyclical downturn. One would actually have to observe what happens to understand the financial implications, as service company reactions to the business cycle can vary quite a bit. Probably the biggest consideration is the service rates that exist during a cyclical downturn. Those rates are often causing a lot of losses (and worse) until the recovery gets underway.

Offshore happens to have an extreme situation, as those projects tend to go to completion once they begin. But many times, new projects in an area will drop to zero for a time for various reasons. This has a much larger impact on the service company (as a rule) than the upstream company.

Summary

Up until now, this company has had a great growth record, which appears to be budgeted to continue into fiscal year 2026. But this is a very low-visibility and volatile industry. Therefore, any budgets have to be taken with "a grain of salt".

That makes that price-earnings ratio that was shown earlier a particularly risky statistic. Clearly there is no cyclical downturn priced into this stock. Yet this is very obviously a cyclical industry. More than once in the past, a growth story has "crashed and burned" when a downcycle began. To me, this company does not look like an exception to the usual story.

I therefore would consider this a strong sell at the current price. Anytime a cyclical stock has a price-earnings ratio above 20 due to the recent growth record, I would consider selling and waiting for the next cyclical downturn to consider getting back in.

This is not a statement about what will happen in the near term with that stock price. Both the aforementioned volatility and low visibility leave all kinds of possible price actions "on the table". It is a statement that the time to get out is when the sun is shining and the forecast is a lot more sunshine in the future.

By the time there is a forecast for rain, the stock price is a whole lot lower than it is now (most likely).

Longtime readers may well remember that Chart Industries (GTLS) reported a quarter with no orders around the 2015 downturn. It is a big reason that Chart diversified away from the industry to the point that the service industry was an "extra" but not a major requirement anymore.

It is also a likely reason that Baker Hughes (BKR) is going to acquire Chart any day now. Baker Hughes has its own diversification program underway. The acquisition of Chart is going to accelerate that diversification in a big way.

Without an industry diversification program, these offshore companies often head to single digits during a cyclical downturn because that is just how extreme those downturns are. I would therefore take my profits and call it a day.

Risks

The situation in Iran calls into question a future projection. Right now, it is more about short-term volatility and low visibility. Literally any outlook is possible right now. To me, this is another reason to avoid this industry.

Finances appear to be in good shape right now. But the pricing during a cyclical downturn is what determines survival to the next recovery. That is a big unknown at the present time. There is always a wide range of possibilities for any part of the service industry. There are always companies that "sail through" with good rates, and others suffer the other extreme. The reasons for this often become too apparent after the information is needed for the average investor.

I think the biggest thing is that a lot of these service companies, like Baker Hughes, are diversifying away from the industry in an attempt to mitigate the harsh nature of the cyclical swings.

This company evidently has not done that (at least yet). To me, that elevates the risk of this investment quite a bit.

data.title: 6 Themes For Earning Season
data.description:
Ready, set, go!

The start of the first-quarter 2026 earnings season is shaping up to be one of the most critical and complex macroeconomic and microeconomic crossroads in recent years for global financial markets. U.S. equities enter the first-quarter 2026 earnings season in a precarious structural balance.

One side of this coin is that investor sentiment has continued to drive tech sector valuations to all-time highs, as large-scale financial commitment to artificial intelligence will be the main driver for these changes. On the other side of this coin, are these valuations very heavily exposed if global geopolitical tensions continue to escalate, such as the Iran situation worsening and disruption in the worldwide energy market?

As a result of extreme volatility and ambiguity regarding the direction of global equity markets, investors have transitioned their focus away from simply whether companies can meet or exceed consensus analyst expectations for the just-concluded quarter. Since the value of exceeding analyst expectations ("the beat") is already incorporated into all valuation models, there exists an increasing obsession among institutional and retail investors toward the long-term sustainability and growth prospects of each company's business model. As such, the upcoming earnings season may serve as the ultimate test for evaluating the resiliency of corporate margin structures in the face of:

  1. An emerging "new" oil and logistics shock;
  2. Capital expenditures associated with infrastructure supporting AI that will begin to be capitalized;
  3. A more selective American consumer than previously anticipated;
  4. Severe weaknesses developing in what are currently opaque segments of the economy, notably, the private credit system.

This article will use an actionable format to dissect the six fundamental areas that will define earnings call discussions over the next several weeks, delivering a comprehensive top-down view with potentially far-reaching effects for individual stock selection.

1. Guidance

The first and likely most important aspect of this reporting period involves the disparity in forecasted results from both analysts and investors. The gap currently appears to have narrowed such that a "beat" no longer represents a buying opportunity but rather a hold/do not sell opportunity. If one were to accelerate their investment based on the current state of the market multiple, then there would need to be an increase in earnings estimates for the next quarter(s) as well as an upward revision in overall 2026 estimates. As it stands now, I believe that estimates are very high, and analyst consensus for the S&P 500 is around 12.6%-13.2% earnings growth and revenue expansion of 9.7% for the 6th straight time, earning double-digit gains, which would represent a record. However, my view is that these estimates may prove somewhat optimistic, particularly if geopolitical risks become a more meaningful headwind than currently anticipated.

S&P 500 Valuation & Growth Metric (Q1 2026) Estimated/Current Value Historical Context and Implications
YoY EPS Growth 12.6% - 13.2% (Potential 19.0%) Sixth straight quarter of double-digit earnings growth. Potentially the strongest growth rate since Q4 2021 if the average positive earnings surprise is applied.
YoY Revenue Growth 9.7%–9.8% Fastest top-line growth pace since Q3 2022.
Positive EPS Guidance Issued 59 companies Well above the 5-year average (44) and the 10-year average (40).
Negative EPS Guidance Issued 51 companies Below the 5-year average (61), pointing to broad pre-release corporate optimism.
Forward P/E Ratio (12 months) 20.4x Meaningfully above the 10-year average (18.9x), leaving very little room for disappointment in forward estimates.

Companies have so far shown an unusual confidence level and have already issued positive guidance from 59 companies, with the tech sector being one of the most active sectors. But the question I raise is, what probability do we really have to maintain EPS forecasts in the mid-teens (around 15-18%) when energy inflation caused by the Iranian conflict just now starts to bite into margins of companies? In my opinion, the market underestimates the need for recalculation. Deutsche Bank was the first to show this when they cut their earnings growth forecast for the Stoxx 600 from 10-12% to 8-10% (Source: latest emailed note "Q2 Outlook - High pressure could lead to high upside"). I believe that many companies will use those calls to remain cautiously defensive, citing Middle Eastern uncertainty as a shield. Ultimately, they do so intentionally: lowering the bar when surprised by unexpected external events such as oil price shocks and then raising it again in subsequent quarters to surprise the market.

2. AI Capex

The second biggest theme of the season is the need for Artificial Intelligence (AI) to continue to evolve. With regard to the Mag7, I have written about it extensively in my prior post that I think you should all go back and read. In general, with respect to the overall IT space, consensus estimates indicate that IT will see profit margins increase by a whopping 45.0%, primarily driven by the semiconductor and semiconductor equipment industry, where estimated earnings growth rates approach nearly 95%, according to FactSet.

As such, the fundamental issue confronting sophisticated investors today has been significantly altered since the previous year. In investing circles, the fundamental question of whether AI represents a structural bullish trend has given way to a far more critical discussion concerning monetization. Precisely, who is the one turning the huge capital expenditure (CAPEX) which is being heavily invested into cash flow at the moment, into a productive use, and who is just piling up extraordinary expense for the purchase of hardware, chips, and computer resources without any clear and immediate return on investment (ROI)? By some estimates, CapEx spending among the biggest hyperscalers (AWS by Amazon, Azure by Microsoft, GCP by Google, and Meta) is expected to exceed $650B in 2026, as you can see below.

These unprecedented levels of infrastructure spending are driving megadeals that are reshaping the hierarchy and competitive positioning in cloud computing, network infrastructure, and core hardware. Ultimately, the market will be brutal in evaluating when each company transitions from pure implementation of infrastructure to generating consistent returns through both software solutions and "Agentic AI", systems capable of autonomously executing complex workflows in lieu of human intervention.

Deals valued at multi-billions have created an extreme divergence among two groups of publicly traded companies: those who have implemented cutting-edge technology and the remaining group of companies producing AI software products whose product utility cannot be quantified. Until there is visibility into the future cash flows of these companies, their price multiples will remain unvalidated. The notion of "visibility of monetization" will severely divide software companies. Many legacy Enterprise Software & Services companies are facing legitimate concerns that they may become obsolete as a result of this dynamic. This has recently caused a sell-now-ask-questions-later cycle in stock markets due to fears that future generations of generative AI platforms and advanced autonomous agents (such as Anthropic's recent "Plugins" implementations for the Claude model) could cannibalize or eliminate entirely existing traditional SaaS business models.

On the other hand, companies that can successfully utilize AI to drive actual and measurable increases in earnings growth will have considerable insulation against these selling pressures and will likely experience multiple expansions, as I said in my last article.

Key Players and AI “Mega-Deals” (2025–2026) Deal Details and Monetization Implications
Oracle (ORCL) Announced a cloud/AI infrastructure mega-deal with a total value of $290 billion in fiscal Q3 2026. This points to an unprecedented ability to monetize large-scale compute infrastructure supply.
Meta Platforms (META)/AMD Signed a massive agreement worth more than $100 billion for GPU supply to support Meta’s AI data centers, highlighting the relentless demand for compute power needed to train next-generation Llama models.
Palantir Technologies (PLTR) Secured a 10-year, $10 billion contract with the U.S. Army, while NATO adopted the Maven system. Palantir is positioning itself as a government and commercial “AI Operating System,” turning its ontology-driven platform into tangible revenue.
Cloudflare (NET) Signed a record $42.5 million annual contract, showing that security and edge infrastructure are critical and highly monetizable components of enterprise-grade AI deployments.

Therefore, during this earnings season, management commentary surrounding timelines associated with Data Center Upgrade Spending Cycles (that include procuring high-density servers, switchgear, high-voltage transformers, and complex liquid cooling systems) and acquiring end customers that are willing to pay a premium for integrating AI into their offerings will receive intense scrutiny. While undeniably strong players exist in the hardware space, such as NVIDIA, Micron (which experienced positive EPS surprises in Q1), and Broadcom, which have shown themselves to be able to take advantage of this explosive spending spree, there is little doubt that the burden of proof is rapidly shifting to software vendors & cloud service providers: they must clearly demonstrate that AI is not simply a colossal cost center if they hope to remain competitive.

3. Oil Shock and Geopolitics

The beginning of 2026 saw a sharp and sudden deterioration in the Middle Eastern geopolitical environment, which was followed by a major interruption of maritime shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Approximately 20% of global oil supply production passes through this area. Therefore, the closure of this strait represented a significant geopolitical shock for all economies and, in my opinion, was even worse than the event linked to the war between Russia and Ukraine, which subsequently caused the closure of pipelines to the European artery (in that case, we're talking about a share of about 16%).

Since the Iranian attacks began late last month, Brent crude oil prices have increased over fifty percent, reaching a high near $115 per barrel. Compared to the same month last year, prices increased by 12.5% in April alone. The quick escalation in crude prices is basically a secret tax on the whole world economy, which decreases the buying power of consumers. At the same time, it compels companies to reduce their profit margins.

Moreover, this rapidly increasing energy price also poses a direct threat to what the Federal Reserve considers the "soft landing" and thus raises concerns regarding a possible stagflationary economic condition that could prompt analysts to lower their interest rate expectations and possibly raise them later in the year. Consequently, for both fundamental analysis and stock picking, the effect of this energy shock will likely be among the most divisive and critical themes of the current earnings season, as it will test whether or not companies truly possess pricing power in an environment where demand is already declining. Energy costs are not merely rising due to higher gasoline prices; they are rising throughout all segments of the global supply chain, affecting transportation costs, maritime and land-based logistics, as well as the cost of industrial raw materials.

Sector Impact of the Energy Shock (April 2026 Estimates) Typical Increase in Operating Costs Estimated Duration of Margin Pressure
Transportation and Logistics 15% - 30% 6-9 months. Immediate impact from higher fuel costs, especially jet fuel and diesel.
Energy-Intensive Manufacturing and Packaging 25% - 50% 9-12 months. Production costs for glass, aluminum, and petrochemical resins used in plastics have surged sharply.
Agricultural Production and Food & Beverage 20% - 35% 12-18 months. Fertilizer-driven inflation, tied to gas and oil derivatives, filters through into agricultural input and food commodity costs.
Consumer Services and Retail 5% - 15% 6-12 months. Higher heating and cooling expenses, along with rising goods transportation costs.

As always, the oil price surge creates an immediate distinction between those industries that benefit from this crisis and those that suffer under it. Companies engaged in the exploration and sale of petroleum products are obviously the primary beneficiaries of this disaster. At the beginning of the crisis, industry earnings were forecast to decline by 9.5% compared to last year. However, after the dramatic increase in oil prices during March, analysts revised their earnings forecasts upward, resulting in a short-term 12.9% increase in forecasts at the beginning of April, prior to partial negative feedback from Exxon Mobil’s guidance (source: FactSet).

Historical financial data, similar to the comparison of historical data to the Gulf War in 1990 and the Ukraine-Russia war in 2022, teaches us that historically only the energy sector has been able to generate generally positive returns and act as a hedge against long-lasting military escalations and blockages of major trade lanes. Basic manufacturing, transportation (particularly airlines whose profit margins will be greatly reduced by the sharply increasing cost of jet fuel), and consumer goods industries will have significant difficulties in maintaining profitability. Specifically, companies in the packaging and wrapping industries that rely heavily upon petrochemical feedstock supplies and require extremely energy-intensive industrial processes will experience a drastic increase in their operating expenses virtually overnight.

Investors will need to carefully review management comments to assess which companies have sufficient pricing power to effectively and expeditiously transfer these price increases onto end customers via list price increases and which will have no choice but to absorb the effects internally, greatly decreasing their operating profits.

4. The American Consumer

Are consumers truly performing or simply becoming even more discerning? The fourth analytical category focuses on the consumer, who accounts for roughly 70 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) as well as being the true driving force behind the U.S. economy. On the surface, high-level aggregate information about job creation, average hourly nominal wages ($36.53 per hour), and other key metrics suggests that the overall performance of the U.S. consumer remains strong and encouraging. However, beneath the surface there is far greater complexity. As evidenced by the recent significant rise in energy prices and subsequent impact on inflation, the consumer price index (CPI) now stands at 3.3 percent year over year, with home-delivered food costs rising 2.7%. Together these factors are dramatically changing how both households and individuals perceive expenses and their willingness to spend money.

For analysis purposes, the appropriate way to view the results of the US consumer sector during the current earnings season should be as a transition from a period of inflationary driven spending and pandemic related savings to an environment of intentional spending where individuals will focus on obtaining the greatest possible value for each dollar they spend and where there will be significantly less price elasticity. US consumers are clearly experiencing a condition known as "uncertainty fatigue" and consequently are directing their disposable income towards fewer but more essential items. A large portion of this decrease in discretionary spending can be attributed to the rapid rise in costs associated with filling up gas pumps and paying higher amounts for household utilities, which serves as a type of "regressively taxable" means of quickly removing liquidity that was once designated for discretionary products such as clothing, electronic devices, and dining out and instead forcing it to be redirected to necessities including groceries, health care, and other essentials. In fact, the most recent edition of The Federal Reserve's "Beige Book" officially documented this trend, noting that in several economic districts, retail sales were dampened due to "economic uncertainty," "increased price sensitivity," and the decline of lower-income consumers' participation in discretionary spending."

5. Banks as an X-ray of the real economy

As has been true since before memory, major U.S. banks (such as JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, Bank of America, and Morgan Stanley) begin reporting for "earnings season" in April. The significance of these banks’ reports extends far beyond the numbers related to the banking sector. Both the quantitative and qualitative information provided by management teams of these banks will serve as a tone-setting mechanism for the entire equity market. This is where both speculation of high finance meets the reality of Main Street.

Loans are expected to grow across every type of loan category in 2026 due to anticipated lower interest rates. Specifically, strong demand is expected in the commercial & industrial (“C&I”) sector, the non-residential real estate (“CRE”) sector, and the residential sector. For example, Wells Fargo anticipates mid-double-digit loan growth on an average basis from the end of 2025 through 2026, primarily driven by auto loans, credit cards, and commercial lending.

However, the overall asset quality trends for 2026 appear more fractured. Wells Fargo anticipates stable trends in Q1 as consumers continue to hold up and maintain pace with inflation-based income growth. Attention, however, will begin to shift to small business and auto loan resiliency. The primary concern is that, if there were to be an economic slowdown or recession, it would likely lead to a cascading number of defaults from the most indebted borrowers.

I believe that current adjusted earnings growth estimates for 2026 are overly optimistic given the underlying deterioration in credit. I am beginning to observe early indicators of strain in agriculture and retail portfolios, while geopolitical uncertainty is already building risks for 2027 in unsecured and SME segments. Another factor creating instability is that deposit growth is still lagging credit growth, leading to high levels of credit-to-deposit ratios, which continue to put pressure on banks’ funding costs.

6. Private credit

After years of aggressive growth, the private credit market is now facing a “refinancing wall” from 2025 to 2026. As many of these loans were originated when interest rates were at zero, they are now being refinanced at a significantly higher rate than a few years ago. Those who do not have access to capital face an unpleasant choice: either default or utilize the increasingly popular tool of “bad PIKs,” where capitalized and unpaid interest was as high as 6.4% of all outstanding loans in Q1 ’26, three times the level of 2022.

For decades, especially in certain industries (such as software with recurring revenue), it was thought that everything was running smoothly. In the first quarter, over $20 billion in redemption requests flooded into the industry. Giants such as Blue Owl, Morgan Stanley, Ares & Apollo were forced to gate their redemptions and restrict the withdrawal of savings from investors. Over the next few weeks we will see a significant strategic divergence within the industry. According to Bloomberg, "JPMorgan Chase & Co. is restricting some lending to private credit funds after marking down the value of certain loans in their portfolios". On the other hand, Goldman Sachs, with better liquidity management than its peers, intends to capitalize upon the retail exodus and shop for the opportunities left vacant by competitors that are faltering. As the Fed and Treasury begin formal examinations of banking and insurance exposures to private credit, this segment could become an important indicator of sentiment toward BDCs and, more broadly, the financial services industry.

Navigating Uncertainty with Sharp Weapons

It will probably not be about just looking at figures during the first quarter of 2026 earnings season. Due to the fact that the S&P 500 trades at high valuation levels, there are no longer margins for error. Investors will have to begin looking behind the "beats" that were experienced in Q1 2026 and focus on whether or not companies can maintain their profit margins as they continue to deal with the effects of the energy shock. Additionally, investors will also want to assess if companies truly have the capability to generate revenue from the large amounts spent on Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology.

Banks are essentially providing an x-ray view of what is rapidly emerging as a two speed economy, and, while private credit has shown some of its first signs of weakness, the mantra for the next few months will be dynamic prudence. In this environment of extreme volatility and changing guidance at a moment's notice, liquidity and operational flexibility will become more than merely options, but essential tools to protect investment portfolios and capitalize on opportunities that will develop due to price movements.

Best of luck with all your investments!

data.title: Ramaco Resources: Brook Mine Optionality Makes It A Viable Rare Earth Play
data.description:
Ramaco Resources, Inc. (METCB) is a natural resource developer company with a dual platform. The core is their metallurgical coal extraction in the Appalachian region, but they also have an emerging Wyoming rare earth and critical minerals project. In my view, its larger long-term opportunity depends on whether or not Brook Mine can evolve into a commercial source of strategic minerals such as gallium, germanium, scandium, and magnet rare earths. And going forward, METCB is reorganizing itself into four divisions for coal, royalty, rare earth, and downstream conversion business. Overall, METCB is undoubtedly a speculative play right now, but given its cheap price tag and strategic optionality, I feel it’s fair to rate it a speculative “Buy” at these levels.

Coal With Strategic Rare Earth Minerals

Ramaco Resources, Inc. is a natural resources company developing metallurgical coal in Central Appalachia and also rare earth and critical minerals in Wyoming. Ramaco was formed back in October 2016, and it's currently headquartered in Lexington, Kentucky. Note that Ramaco has two share classes. METC is the Class A common stock associated with the metallurgical coal business. METCB is tied to royalty and infrastructure income and future rare earth optionality. Specifically, I previously covered METCB last June, and shortly after, the stock appreciated significantly. Since then, the METCB has pulled back, which is why I felt it was worthwhile updating my thesis on this name.

As a quick recap, METCB’s investments include royalty income from coal reserves mined mainly by Ramaco. This means that METCB shareholders don’t benefit directly from operating Ramaco’s mines. Additionally, METCB receives fee-based income from Ramaco’s infrastructure, such as plants and rails. But, I believe their main appeal lies with METCB’s potential future royalties from the rare earth elements (REEs) deposit if it's successfully developed at some point.

You see, national production of rare earth minerals and critical materials is of considerable strategic value for the US. These minerals are vital for missile and radar systems, aircraft components, and advanced electronics, among other applications. Recently, China restricted the export of gallium, germanium, and scandium used in semiconductors, fiber optics, and other industrial and defense applications. In fact, China’s restrictions affect the supply chain for manufacturers in the US and highlight why it’s critical for the US to secure its own mining and processing capacity to reduce its dependency on imports.

Additionally, the International Energy Agency believes demand for REEs may increase threefold to sevenfold by 2040. Particularly, secular trends across electric vehicles, advanced electronics, and energy transition infrastructure rely heavily on rare earth magnets, which should act as long-term secular tailwinds for METCB. Management also mentioned that the rising demand for rare earth minerals only strengthens the case for reducing the US’s dependency on China's exports and developing domestic production.

Therefore, generally speaking, this strategic context is likely to benefit from US policy support and funding opportunities. And, fortunately, Ramaco has a promising mine for rare earths and critical minerals with its Brook Mine project. So far, Ramaco has developed a processing design for better recovery of targeted materials using carbochlorination as the dominant technology. This method is already used in much of commercial titanium processing. Ramaco plans to sell magnetic rare earth production as a mixed rare earth carbonate (MREC), an intermediate product.

This approach for producing MRECs reduces capital intensity, which would be required to fully refine everything on-site and produce each rare earth mineral separately. Ramaco’s processing would theoretically stop at the MREC stage, with multiple rare earth elements combined. Its output would include magnet rare earths such as Neodymium, Praseodymium, Dysprosium, and Terbium. But after that, further processing can yield separate oxides and then potentially other metals, alloys, and magnets.

Furthermore, Ramaco’s Brook Mine has some advantages and features that make it different from other similar projects. The first advantage is that most of the estimated rare earth and critical minerals are found mingled in coal, shale, and clay, which makes mining them less expensive than processing them from hard rock. Secondly, Ramaco’s Brook Mine has significant deposits of gallium, germanium, and scandium, plus percentages of magnetic and heavy rare earths, while other projects obtain mainly lower-value mixes.

Their third advantage with the Brook Mine is that this mine is already permitted and officially opened (July 2025). After all, permitting is typically an annoying bottleneck for mining projects. Lastly, the fourth advantage is that, according to Fluor (FLR), Ramaco’s Brook Mine is the only primary mine source for gallium, germanium, and scandium in the world. Other producers may supply these materials, but they are often recovered as byproducts from other industrial processes, and they don’t have mines built to extract these materials.

Long-Term Strategic Value

Therefore, as a whole, Ramaco’s Brook Mine is a rather unique property, and more importantly, a potential major rare earth source for the US in the long run. It's estimated to contain 1.4 million in-place rare earth oxide (REO) tons with primary magnet rare earths plus gallium, germanium, and scandium. And this mine may have a mix of recoverable minerals, which means that Ramaco can potentially extract more money per ton of ore than other mixes found across comparable projects. Ramaco estimates its mix’s price per ton could be over 15 times higher than its peer’s average.

On top of that, Ramaco reorganized its activities into four divisions. The first one is Metallurgical Coal Production and Sales for the traditional met coal business. The Division 2 is Rare Earth and Critical Mineral Development and Sales, which includes the Brook Mine and the company’s western rare earth, critical mineral exploration and development, and other thermal operations. The third division is Royalty and Infrastructure, which owns the assets and receives payments from operating divisions for the use of the assets. Division 4 is Critical Mineral Refining and Processing, which would handle the future conversion of mineral feedstock into rare earth and critical minerals using the carbochlorination technology.

But what stood out for me is that Ramaco said that one or more divisions could potentially access markets separately in the future. In my view, this also hints that Ramaco may be well-positioned for exploring M&A opportunities in the future as well. For example, I imagine there could be buyers interested in Ramaco’s Brook Mine project precisely due to its unique strategic appeal with rare earth minerals. And, while this is still purely hypothetical, an M&A deal for this mine should quickly unlock substantial shareholder value.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, METCB currently trades at an $874.3 million market cap. Its latest 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $440.4 million in cash against $451.4 million in debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). Based on those figures, I estimate METCB’s EV at roughly $885.3 million. And the analysts’ consensus projects METCB will generate $725.2 million in revenues by 2027, which would price the stock at a compelling forward EV/S of 1.2. For comparison, the sector’s median is 1.6, so I do believe METCB is probably at a slight discount at these levels.

Nevertheless, I estimate METCB burned through $31.1 million during Q4 2025 alone. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. This suggests an elevated annualized burn rate of $124.4 million, implying a cash runway of only 3.5 years. I reckon this seems manageable for now. However, these cash flow figures are a huge -$34.6 million YoY cash flow swing, as METCB generated a slightly positive cash flow of $3.3 million in Q4 2024 (using the same calculation method). In that sense, I think this is easily METCB’s main risk at this point, because it’s steadily draining its otherwise liquid balance sheet.

However, note that METCB stock can certainly appreciate quickly, as it rallied 259.0% from its 2025 lows to a little over $20.00 a share. At the time, several tailwinds coalesced and helped re-rate the stock rapidly higher. At the time, METCB basically confirmed that their Brook Mine was “commercially and technologically” viable, which could be a huge catalyst for them as a long-term revenue vertical. And shortly after, they raised $200.0 million through a public offering precisely to accelerate the development of its properties. So, the future undoubtedly seemed quite promising just a few months ago for METCB.

Unfortunately, since then, the stock has come down significantly, and it’s now 51.0% off from those 2025 highs. And it’s mostly because those same 2025 narrative tailwinds have now flipped against METCB. In fact, METCB’s 10-K report explicitly warns that they can give no assurances about being able to actually develop their Brook Mine property into a commercial-scale mine. At the same time, the macro picture weakened for METCB, as metallurgical coal prices softened due to ample supply and lower steel production.

But, looking forward, I think METCB now has a much more compelling valuation yet again. Naturally, they have to prove that their Brook Mine can indeed become a product asset. And my bull case rests almost entirely on this assumption. Because the coal side of their business isn’t as exciting in my view. It’s the strategic value of their rare minerals and relatively cheap price tag that ultimately nudges me towards a speculative “Buy” at these levels. This is especially true if geopolitical tensions persist or worsen, which could make rare minerals for defensive applications and batteries extremely valuable.

Conclusion: Worth Speculating Long-Term

Overall, I think METCB has retraced enough to make it a viable speculative “Buy” again. Naturally, it’s a thinly traded stock with only 39.4 thousand shares in average volume, which, at the current $10.25 PPS, implies a shallow trading liquidity of only $403.9 thousand. So the stock will likely remain volatile in the near term. Fortunately, they’ve continued paying quarterly dividends for METCB, and in Q1 2026, they distributed $0.1489 per share (paid in stock), which is equivalent to a 5.8% yield right now. That’s why, on balance, I feel long-term investors at these levels have a reasonably favorable risk-reward play on METCB’s latent rare earth minerals in the Brook Mine project.
